title: Subject matter
chunk_text: Article 1 sets out the purpose of MiCA. The Regulation establishes uniform requirements for the offer to the public and admission to trading on a trading platform of crypto-assets that are not already covered by other Union financial services legislation. It distinguishes three categories of crypto-asset: asset-referenced tokens, e-money tokens, and other crypto-assets. The Regulation lays down requirements for issuers and offerors of these crypto-assets and for crypto-asset service providers, covering transparency and disclosure for offers and admission to trading; authorisation and supervision of crypto-asset service providers, issuers of asset-referenced tokens, and issuers of e-money tokens, as well as their operation, organisation and governance; consumer/holder protection in connection with the offer, admission to trading and provision of services; and measures to prevent insider dealing, unlawful disclosure of inside information and market manipulation in respect of crypto-assets, in order to ensure market integrity.

title: Scope
chunk_text: Article 2 defines MiCA's scope and its exclusions. The Regulation applies to natural and legal persons and certain other undertakings that are engaged in the issuance, offer to the public and admission to trading of crypto-assets, or that provide crypto-asset services in the Union. It does not apply to crypto-assets that are unique and not fungible with other crypto-assets (non-fungible tokens, including digital art and collectibles), though the substance rather than the label is decisive and fractional or large-series NFTs may still fall in scope. MiCA also does not apply to crypto-assets that already qualify as financial instruments, deposits, structured deposits, funds (except where they are e-money tokens), securitisation positions, insurance or pension products, and similar instruments already covered by existing Union financial services law. Certain public entities such as the ECB, national central banks acting as monetary authorities, the EIB and public international organisations are excluded, as are purely internal or fully decentralised services with no intermediary.

title: Definitions
chunk_text: Article 3(1)(5) defines 'crypto-asset' as a digital representation of a value or of a right that is able to be transferred and stored electronically using distributed ledger technology or similar technology. This is the foundational concept of MiCA: it is deliberately broad and technology-based, capturing tokens regardless of their economic function, and it underpins the Regulation's three sub-categories of asset-referenced tokens, e-money tokens, and other crypto-assets. The definition turns on (i) a digital representation of value or rights, (ii) transferability and storage by electronic means, and (iii) reliance on distributed ledger technology (DLT) or comparable technology. Crypto-assets that are unique and non-fungible, or that already qualify as financial instruments or other regulated products, fall outside MiCA by virtue of Article 2 even though they may otherwise meet this definition.

title: Definitions
chunk_text: Article 3(1)(6) defines 'asset-referenced token' (ART) as a type of crypto-asset that is not an e-money token and that purports to maintain a stable value by referencing another value or right, or a combination of these, including one or more official currencies. ARTs are therefore stablecoin-like instruments backed by a basket or by assets other than a single official currency: for example, a token referencing several fiat currencies, one or more commodities, one or more other crypto-assets, or a mix of these. Because they can reference multiple assets or currencies, ARTs are treated as the more complex stablecoin category and are subject to the stricter authorisation, reserve, governance and disclosure (white paper) requirements set out in Title III of MiCA. A token that references only a single official currency is instead classified as an e-money token under Article 3(1)(7), not an ART.

title: Definitions
chunk_text: Article 3(1)(7) defines 'e-money token' (EMT) as a type of crypto-asset that purports to maintain a stable value by referencing the value of one official currency. EMTs are single-fiat-currency stablecoins (for example a token pegged to the euro or the US dollar) and are treated under MiCA as functionally close to electronic money. They are regulated under Title IV: an EMT may in principle be issued only by an authorised credit institution or electronic money institution, holders have a right of redemption at par value at any time, and the issuer must publish a crypto-asset white paper. Because an EMT references exactly one official currency, it is distinguished from an asset-referenced token (Article 3(1)(6)), which references other values, rights or a combination, including baskets of currencies or assets.

title: Definitions
chunk_text: Article 3(1)(15) defines 'crypto-asset service provider' (CASP) as a legal person or other undertaking whose occupation or business is the provision of one or more crypto-asset services to clients on a professional basis, and that is allowed to provide crypto-asset services in accordance with MiCA. In practice a CASP must be authorised under Title V of the Regulation (or be a qualifying credit institution or other regulated entity that has notified its intention to provide such services), must have its registered office and place of effective management in a Member State, and is subject to ongoing prudential, organisational, governance, conduct-of-business and consumer-protection requirements. The reference to professional provision to clients distinguishes CASPs from persons acting purely for their own account or on a non-professional, occasional basis. The specific services that, when provided professionally, trigger CASP status are listed in Article 3(1)(16).

title: Definitions
chunk_text: Article 3(1)(16) defines 'crypto-asset service' as any of the following services and activities relating to any crypto-asset: (a) providing custody and administration of crypto-assets on behalf of clients; (b) operation of a trading platform for crypto-assets; (c) exchange of crypto-assets for funds; (d) exchange of crypto-assets for other crypto-assets; (e) execution of orders for crypto-assets on behalf of clients; (f) placing of crypto-assets; (g) reception and transmission of orders for crypto-assets on behalf of clients; (h) providing advice on crypto-assets; (i) providing portfolio management on crypto-assets; and (j) providing transfer services for crypto-assets on behalf of clients. Performing one or more of these services on a professional basis for clients is what makes a person a crypto-asset service provider under Article 3(1)(15) and subjects them to authorisation and conduct requirements under Title V of MiCA.

title: Offers to the public of crypto-assets other than asset-referenced tokens or e-money tokens
chunk_text: Before offering a crypto-asset (other than an asset-referenced token or e-money token) to the public in the Union, or seeking its admission to trading, an offeror or person seeking admission must be a legal person, draw up a crypto-asset white paper, notify it to the competent authority, and publish it; marketing communications must also be drawn up and published. Several exemptions apply: no white paper is required where the crypto-asset is offered for free, is automatically created as a reward for maintaining a distributed ledger or validating transactions, is a utility token giving access to a good or service that already exists, or can only be used for limited goods/services from the issuer's network of merchants. Notably, no white paper is needed if, over 12 months, the offer is made to fewer than 150 natural or legal persons per Member State; the total consideration does not exceed EUR 1 000 000; or the offer is solely to qualified investors who can only hold it themselves. Admission to trading still triggers obligations even where an offer was exempt.

title: Content and form of the crypto-asset white paper
chunk_text: The crypto-asset white paper must contain prescribed information set out in MiCA's Annex I, organised into sections covering: the offeror, person seeking admission, or operator of the trading platform; the issuer (if different); the crypto-asset project; the offer to the public or admission to trading; the crypto-asset itself; the rights and obligations attached to it; the underlying technology; the risks; and the principal adverse impacts on the climate and environment of the consensus mechanism. All information must be fair, clear and not misleading, complete, and free of material omissions, and must be presented in a concise and comprehensible form. The white paper must include a prominent statement that it has not been approved by any competent authority and a summary giving key information in non-technical language. It must be dated, contain a table of contents, and be drawn up in an official language of the home Member State (or a language customary in international finance). It is made available in machine-readable format, and must include specified mandatory statements and a liability attestation by the management body.

title: Marketing communications
chunk_text: Any marketing communications relating to an offer to the public of a crypto-asset (other than an asset-referenced token or e-money token) or to its admission to trading must satisfy several conditions. They must be clearly identifiable as marketing communications; the information they contain must be fair, clear and not misleading; and it must be consistent with the information in the crypto-asset white paper. Marketing communications must state that a crypto-asset white paper has been published and give the website address of the offeror or person seeking admission to trading, together with a telephone number and email address to contact them. They must also contain a clear and prominent statement warning that holders may face a total or partial loss of the value of the funds invested, or that the crypto-asset may not always be transferable or liquid. Marketing communications must not be disseminated before the white paper is published, and must be made available, on request, to the competent authority. Where a white paper is not required, marketing communications must still meet equivalent fairness conditions.

title: Notification of the crypto-asset white paper and of the marketing communications
chunk_text: Offerors, persons seeking admission to trading, or operators of trading platforms must notify their crypto-asset white paper to the competent authority of their home Member State. Notification must occur at least 20 working days before the date of publication of the white paper. The competent authority does not approve the white paper before publication; there is no prior-approval regime. Together with the white paper, the notifying person must provide an explanation of why the crypto-asset should not be classified as an excluded crypto-asset, an asset-referenced token, or an e-money token, plus a list of any host Member States where they intend to offer the asset or seek admission, and the related starting dates. On request, marketing communications must also be notified. Competent authorities may require amendments. After publication, the white paper and marketing communications must be made available, and ESMA maintains a register of published white papers. The notification mechanism allows the offer or admission to take effect across the Union without separate authorisation in host Member States.

title: Right of withdrawal
chunk_text: Retail holders who acquire a crypto-asset (other than an asset-referenced token or e-money token) directly from an offeror, or from a crypto-asset service provider placing the asset on the offeror's behalf, have a right of withdrawal. They may withdraw their agreement to purchase, free of charge and without giving reasons, within 14 calendar days. This cooling-off period runs from the date of the holder's agreement to purchase. During that period any payment, including charges, must be reimbursed without undue delay and within 14 days of being informed of the withdrawal, using the same means of payment originally used. The right of withdrawal does not apply where the crypto-asset is already admitted to trading at the time of purchase, nor where the offer had a fixed subscription period that has ended. The offeror must include information about this right of withdrawal in its crypto-asset white paper. Where this withdrawal right applies, the withdrawal right under the distance marketing of financial services regime does not apply.

title: Liability of offerors or persons seeking admission to trading for the information given in a crypto-asset white paper
chunk_text: Where an offeror, person seeking admission to trading, or operator of a trading platform, or a member of their administrative, management or supervisory body, has given information in their crypto-asset white paper (including any modified version) that is not complete, fair, or clear, or that is misleading, that person and its body members are civilly liable to a holder of the crypto-asset for any loss caused by that infringement. A contractual exclusion or limitation of this civil liability is deprived of legal effect. The holder bears responsibility for presenting evidence indicating that the offeror or relevant person breached the white paper requirements through such information and that this breach had an impact on the holder's decision to buy, sell, or exchange the crypto-asset. The summary of the white paper, including any translation, does not by itself give rise to civil liability unless it is misleading, inaccurate, or inconsistent with the other parts of the white paper, or fails to provide key information to assist holders. This regime is without prejudice to other civil liability under national law.

title: Authorisation
chunk_text: A person may not offer an asset-referenced token (ART) to the public, or seek its admission to trading, within the Union unless it is the issuer and has been authorised by the competent authority of its home Member State, or is a credit institution complying with Article 17. The issuer must be a legal person or other undertaking established in the Union. An exemption applies where, over 12 months, the average outstanding value of the ART does not exceed EUR 5 000 000 (or a higher threshold set by a Member State), or where the ART is offered solely to qualified investors and only such investors can hold it. Authorised issuers must publish an approved crypto-asset white paper. Authorisation is valid throughout the Union and allows the issuer to offer the ART across the EU or seek admission to trading.

title: Specific requirements for credit institutions
chunk_text: A credit institution may offer an asset-referenced token to the public or seek its admission to trading without obtaining the authorisation under Article 16, provided it draws up a crypto-asset white paper, submits it for approval to the competent authority of its home Member State, and obtains that approval. Before doing so, the credit institution must notify its competent authority at least 90 working days in advance, providing specified information including the programme of operations, a legal opinion that the ART is not an e-money token or otherwise excluded, the governance arrangements, and the policies required under Title III. The competent authority assesses the information and may object. The provisions of Title III on reserve assets, redemption, own funds, governance and related obligations continue to apply to the credit institution, but the prudential authorisation requirement is replaced by the white paper approval and notification process.

title: Modification of published crypto-asset white papers for asset-referenced tokens
chunk_text: Issuers of asset-referenced tokens must notify the competent authority of their home Member State of any intended change to their business model likely to have a significant influence on the purchase decision of any holder or prospective holder, or any change to an already published crypto-asset white paper. Such changes must be described in a draft modified white paper. The issuer informs the competent authority of the intended modification at least 30 working days before the proposed date of the change. The competent authority may require modifications, suspensions, or additional information, and assesses the modified white paper, in principle approving or refusing it within a set period. Where the change could affect the protection of holders or the integrity of the market, holders must be given relevant information. The modified white paper, with clearly marked changes, must be published, and timelines for assessment and approval mirror those for the original white paper.

title: Governance arrangements
chunk_text: Issuers of asset-referenced tokens must have robust governance arrangements, including a clear organisational structure with well-defined, transparent and consistent lines of responsibility, effective processes to identify, manage, monitor and report the risks they are or might be exposed to, and adequate internal control mechanisms, including sound administrative and accounting procedures. Members of the management body must be of sufficiently good repute and possess the appropriate knowledge, skills and experience, individually and collectively, and must commit sufficient time to perform their duties. Persons holding qualifying shareholdings must also be of good repute. Issuers must adopt policies and procedures that are effective to ensure compliance with the Regulation, including business continuity policies, internal audit, and arrangements for the security and integrity of ICT systems. Governance arrangements must be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the ART and the issuer's activities, and be documented and kept up to date.

title: Own funds requirements
chunk_text: Issuers of asset-referenced tokens must at all times hold own funds equal to at least the highest of: EUR 350 000; 2 % of the average amount of the reserve of assets; or one quarter of the fixed overheads of the preceding year. The own funds consist of Common Equity Tier 1 items and instruments, after specified deductions, and must be free of encumbrances. The competent authority may require an issuer to hold up to 20 % more own funds where warranted by factors such as a higher degree of risk, the results of a stress-testing programme, or deficiencies in risk management or internal controls; conversely it may permit up to 20 % less in lower-risk situations. Issuers must regularly conduct stress testing that accounts for severe but plausible financial and non-financial stress scenarios, and the competent authority may impose additional own funds requirements based on the outcome.

title: Obligation to have a reserve of assets, and composition and management of such reserve of assets
chunk_text: Issuers of asset-referenced tokens must constitute and at all times maintain a reserve of assets to cover the risks associated with the assets referenced. The reserve must be composed and managed so that the risks relating to the referenced assets are covered and the liquidity risks linked to the permanent right of redemption of holders are addressed. The reserve of assets must be legally segregated from the issuer's own estate and from the reserves of other tokens, held in the interests of the holders, and insulated so that holders' claims are protected, in particular in the event of insolvency. The reserve must be operationally and legally separated, managed to ensure that issuance and redemption are always matched by corresponding increases or decreases in the reserve, and the aggregate value (measured at market prices) must at least equal the aggregate value of holders' claims. Issuers must have a clear and detailed policy describing the stabilisation mechanism, and the reserve is subject to a regular independent audit.

title: Custody of reserve assets
chunk_text: Issuers of asset-referenced tokens must establish, maintain and implement custody policies, procedures and contractual arrangements ensuring that reserve assets are segregated from the issuer's own assets, are not encumbered or pledged as collateral, and are promptly accessible. Reserve assets must be held in custody no later than five working days after the ART is issued. Crypto-asset reserve assets must be held by a crypto-asset service provider authorised for custody; other reserve assets (financial instruments, cash) must be held by a credit institution or, for financial instruments, an investment firm. Custodians are appointed under a written contract, must act honestly, fairly, professionally, independently and in the interests of the holders, and must avoid conflicts of interest. The custodian is liable to the issuer and the holders for loss of reserve assets held in custody, unless it proves the loss resulted from an external event beyond its reasonable control. Issuers must exercise due skill, care and diligence in selecting, appointing and reviewing custodians.

title: Investment of the reserve of assets
chunk_text: Issuers that invest part of the reserve of assets may invest only in highly liquid financial instruments with minimal market risk, credit risk and concentration risk. The investments must be capable of being liquidated rapidly with minimal adverse price effect. Investments must be made in financial instruments denominated in the same currency as the one referenced by the ART, or in a way that does not introduce undue currency mismatch. Assets resulting from the investment of the reserve must be held in custody in accordance with the custody rules. All profits or losses, including fluctuations in value and any counterparty or operational risks arising from the investment, are borne by the issuer. Issuers must take into account the overall risk profile of the reserve assets and ensure that investment of the reserve does not impair their ability to honour redemption requests. Where the investment results in losses, these must be absorbed by the issuer's own funds.

title: Right of redemption
chunk_text: Holders of asset-referenced tokens have a permanent right of redemption against the issuer at all times. The issuer must establish, maintain and implement clear and detailed policies and procedures regarding this right. Upon a holder's request, the issuer must redeem either by paying an amount in funds, other than electronic money, equal to the market value of the assets referenced by the ART, or by delivering the referenced assets. The issuer sets out the conditions of redemption, including any fees, which must be proportionate to the actual costs incurred and must not act as a deterrent to redemption. Redemption must be possible at all times and the right may not be subject to a fee that is disproportionate. Where the value of the reserve is insufficient to satisfy redemption requests, holders' claims rank accordingly. The redemption rights, conditions and procedures must be disclosed in the crypto-asset white paper.

title: Additional obligations for issuers of significant asset-referenced tokens
chunk_text: Issuers of asset-referenced tokens classified as significant are subject to enhanced obligations. They must adopt and implement a remuneration policy that promotes sound and effective risk management and does not create incentives to relax risk standards. They must ensure that any reserve of assets can be held in custody, where applicable, by different custodians, and they must assess and monitor liquidity needs to meet redemption requests, including establishing, maintaining and implementing a liquidity management policy. They are subject to a higher own funds requirement of 3 % (rather than 2 %) of the average reserve of assets. They must conduct stress testing more frequently and regularly, taking into account the higher risk. Following such stress tests, and depending on the outcome and the issuer's overall financial situation, the competent authority (the EBA, which supervises significant ARTs) may impose stricter own funds requirements or other prudential measures. These additional obligations reflect the systemic importance and scale of significant ARTs.

title: Requirements for the offer to the public or admission to trading of e-money tokens
chunk_text: No e-money token may be offered to the public in the EU, or admitted to trading, unless its issuer is authorised either as a credit institution or as an electronic money institution, and unless the issuer has drawn up a crypto-asset white paper and notified it to the competent authority. The white paper must be published on the issuer's website. E-money tokens are deemed to be electronic money and must comply with the relevant provisions of Directive 2009/110/EC, save where Title IV provides otherwise. Limited exemptions apply: the public-offer requirements do not bind issuers where the token is offered only to qualified investors or where the average outstanding value over twelve months stays below a low threshold, though such issuers must still notify and produce a white paper. Holders of e-money tokens must be granted a claim on the issuer. Marketing communications must be fair, clear and not misleading and consistent with the white paper.

title: Issuance and redeemability of e-money tokens
chunk_text: Issuers of e-money tokens must issue them at par value on receipt of funds. Each holder is granted a direct claim against the issuer, and the tokens must be redeemable at any time and at par value, in funds (other than electronic money) denominated in the official currency the token references, upon the holder's request. The terms of redemption, including any conditions, must be stated prominently in the crypto-asset white paper. Redemption must be carried out without charging a fee. Where a crypto-asset service provider distributes e-money tokens on behalf of an issuer, that distribution does not relieve the issuer of these obligations. Any contractual arrangement that derogates from the right to redemption at par value is prohibited and void.

title: Prohibition of granting interest in relation to e-money tokens
chunk_text: Issuers of e-money tokens are prohibited from granting interest in relation to those tokens. Likewise, crypto-asset service providers are prohibited from granting interest when providing services relating to e-money tokens. For these purposes, any remuneration or other benefit is treated as interest where it is related to the length of time during which a holder holds the e-money token, including compensation or discounts with an effect equivalent to interest, whether provided by the issuer itself or by third parties. This prohibition is intended to preserve the function of e-money tokens as a means of exchange rather than a store of value yielding a return, mirroring the treatment of electronic money under EU law.

title: Investment of funds received in exchange for e-money tokens
chunk_text: Where issuers of e-money tokens invest the funds received in exchange for the tokens, those funds must be invested only in secure, low-risk assets that qualify as highly liquid financial instruments with minimal market risk, credit risk and concentration risk. The investments must be denominated in the same official currency as the one referenced by the e-money token, so as to avoid currency mismatch risk. The assets must be capable of being liquidated rapidly with minimal adverse price effect. This requirement complements the safeguarding obligations applicable under Directive 2009/110/EC governing electronic money institutions, ensuring that funds backing e-money tokens are protected and readily available to meet redemption requests at par value on demand.

title: Additional obligations for issuers of significant e-money tokens
chunk_text: Issuers of e-money tokens classified as significant are subject to additional obligations beyond those applying to ordinary e-money tokens. They must comply with certain enhanced requirements that otherwise apply to asset-referenced tokens, including rules on remuneration policy, interoperability, liquidity management, and a higher own-funds requirement, as well as obligations to assess and monitor liquidity needs. Significant e-money tokens denominated in a non-EU official currency are additionally subject to specific measures. Where a significant e-money token is involved, supervision shifts in part to the European Banking Authority, which exercises supervisory powers over the issuer. Issuers must put in place a liquidity management policy and conduct stress testing, and may be required by the EBA to hold a higher amount of own funds. These measures reflect the greater potential impact of significant tokens on financial stability and monetary sovereignty.

title: Authorisation
chunk_text: No person may provide crypto-asset services within the Union unless that person is either (a) a legal person or other undertaking that has been authorised as a crypto-asset service provider (CASP) in accordance with Article 63, or (b) a credit institution, central securities depository, investment firm, market operator, electronic money institution, UCITS management company or alternative investment fund manager permitted to provide such services under Article 60. CASPs must have a registered office in a Member State where they carry out at least part of their business and have their place of effective management in the Union. At least one director must be resident in the Union. A legal person established outside the Union may not solicit clients in the Union except where the service is provided at the exclusive initiative of the client (reverse solicitation), which does not entitle that third-country firm to market other services. Member States must not impose additional requirements on authorised CASPs. ESMA maintains a public register of CASPs.

title: Provision of crypto-asset services by certain financial entities
chunk_text: Certain already-regulated financial entities may provide crypto-asset services without obtaining a fresh CASP authorisation, provided they notify their competent authority. Credit institutions may provide any crypto-asset service; central securities depositories may provide custody and administration; investment firms may provide services equivalent to those they are already authorised for under MiFID II; electronic money institutions may provide custody and transfer services for the e-money tokens they issue; UCITS management companies and AIFMs may provide services equivalent to their existing permissions (such as reception and transmission of orders, advice and portfolio management). At least 40 working days before first providing services, the entity must notify its competent authority with prescribed information (programme of operations, internal controls, ICT and security arrangements, segregation procedures, etc.). The authority assesses completeness within set timeframes. These entities remain subject to the relevant operating-condition and conduct obligations of Title V. ESMA is informed and the entities are entered in the register.

title: Application for authorisation as a crypto-asset service provider
chunk_text: An applicant for CASP authorisation must submit an application to the competent authority of its home Member State containing extensive prescribed information. This includes the applicant's name, legal form, identifiers and address; a programme of operations setting out the crypto-asset services intended to be provided and how; proof of meeting the prudential safeguards under Article 67; a description of governance arrangements, internal control mechanisms, risk-assessment and business-continuity procedures; the identity of natural persons in the management body and of shareholders or members with qualifying holdings, together with evidence of their good repute and competence; descriptions of ICT systems and security arrangements; segregation and safekeeping procedures for clients' crypto-assets and funds; complaints-handling procedures; and, for specific services, additional information (e.g. operating rules for a trading platform, custody policy, conflict-of-interest and order-execution policies). ESMA, in cooperation with EBA, develops technical standards specifying the content and format. Competent authorities may request further information during assessment.

title: Prudential requirements
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers must at all times hold prudential safeguards at least equal to the higher of (a) the permanent minimum capital requirement set according to the type of services provided, as specified in Annex IV, or (b) one quarter of the fixed overheads of the preceding year, reviewed annually. The Annex IV minimum capital amounts are tiered by service class: EUR 50 000 for providers offering services such as execution, placement, reception and transmission of orders, advice, portfolio management or transfer services; EUR 125 000 for custody and administration, or for exchange of crypto-assets for funds or other crypto-assets; and EUR 150 000 for operating a trading platform. The prudential safeguards may take the form of own funds, an insurance policy covering the relevant risks, or a comparable guarantee, or a combination thereof. Where a CASP has not been in business for one year, it uses projected fixed overheads from its business plan. EBA, in cooperation with ESMA, develops technical standards on the calculation and on the minimum insurance-policy features.

title: Governance arrangements
chunk_text: Members of the management body of a crypto-asset service provider must be of sufficiently good repute and possess, individually and collectively, the appropriate knowledge, skills and experience to perform their duties, and must commit sufficient time. They must not have been convicted of relevant offences (such as money laundering, terrorist financing or other financial crimes). Shareholders and members with direct or indirect qualifying holdings must also be of good repute. CASPs must adopt robust governance arrangements, including a clear organisational structure with well-defined, transparent and consistent lines of responsibility, effective risk-management and internal control mechanisms, and sound administrative and accounting procedures. They must employ adequate human and technical resources, maintain business-continuity policies, and have ICT systems and security arrangements compliant with the EU Digital Operational Resilience framework. They must keep records of all services, activities, orders and transactions. Competent authorities must be able to assess and supervise these arrangements, which must be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the services provided. EBA and ESMA may issue further guidelines.

title: Holding of clients' crypto-assets and funds
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers that hold clients' crypto-assets or the means of access to them must make adequate arrangements to safeguard clients' ownership rights, particularly in the event of the provider's insolvency, and to prevent the use of those crypto-assets for their own account. Clients' crypto-assets must be segregated from the CASP's own holdings. Where a CASP holds funds belonging to clients (other than e-money tokens), it must promptly place those funds with a central bank or a credit institution and safeguard them, keeping them segregated from the provider's own funds. Such client funds must not be used by the provider for its own account. CASPs may deposit client funds with a credit institution or central bank only under arrangements that protect clients' rights. The provider must ensure that client crypto-assets and funds are held in a way that they can be readily distinguished from its own assets and from those of other clients, with appropriate records maintained.

title: Complaints-handling procedures
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers must establish and maintain effective and transparent procedures for the prompt, fair and consistent handling of complaints received from clients, and must publish descriptions of those procedures. Clients must be able to file complaints free of charge. CASPs must maintain a record of all complaints received and of the measures taken to resolve them. They must investigate all complaints in a timely and fair manner and communicate the outcome to complainants within a reasonable period. The procedures must enable clients to submit complaints in an official language of the Member State where the services are marketed, or in another agreed language. Complaints handling must be carried out without prejudice to clients' rights to bring legal proceedings or to seek redress through alternative dispute-resolution mechanisms or competent authorities. ESMA, in cooperation with EBA, develops draft regulatory technical standards specifying the requirements, templates and procedures for handling complaints.

title: Identification, prevention, management and disclosure of conflicts of interest
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers must implement and maintain effective policies and procedures, proportionate to their size and activities, to identify, prevent, manage and disclose conflicts of interest. Such conflicts may arise between the provider (including its managers, employees, tied agents, or any person directly or indirectly linked to it by control) and its clients, or between one client and another. Where organisational or administrative arrangements are insufficient to ensure, with reasonable confidence, that risks of damage to clients' interests will be prevented, the CASP must clearly disclose to clients, before undertaking business on their behalf, the general nature and sources of the conflict and the steps taken to mitigate it. The disclosure must be made on a durable medium and be sufficiently detailed, taking into account the client's nature, to enable an informed decision. CASPs must review their conflict-of-interest policy at least annually and take appropriate measures to address deficiencies. ESMA may develop technical standards on the content and procedures for disclosure.

title: Provision of custody and administration of crypto-assets on behalf of clients
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers that provide custody and administration of crypto-assets must enter into a written agreement with each client setting out their duties and responsibilities, including the means of access, security systems used, types of crypto-assets covered, and the custody policy. They must keep a register of positions opened in each client's name, maintain an internal record of clients' rights, and keep client crypto-assets segregated from the provider's own holdings. CASPs must establish a custody policy with internal rules and procedures to safeguard such assets or the means of access. They must facilitate the exercise of clients' rights and must not use clients' crypto-assets for their own account without express consent. At least quarterly, and on request, the provider must give each client a statement of positions. CASPs are liable to clients for the loss of crypto-assets or means of access resulting from incidents attributable to them, up to the market value of the crypto-assets lost. The provider must maintain an appropriate register of positions.

title: Operation of a trading platform for crypto-assets
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers operating a trading platform must lay down, maintain and implement clear and transparent operating rules, including the approval process for admitting crypto-assets to trading, any exclusion categories, and policies and fees. They must not admit crypto-assets that have an inbuilt anonymisation function unless holders and transaction history can be identified, and must perform due diligence on each crypto-asset admitted. Operators must not deal on own account, including matched principal trading, except where they execute orders on a non-discretionary basis. Their systems must be resilient, have sufficient capacity, ensure orderly trading under stressed conditions, be fully tested, and subject to effective business continuity. They must have transparent, non-discriminatory rules based on objective criteria governing access, and arrangements to identify and manage disruption. They must make public bid and ask prices and the depth of trading interest (pre-trade transparency) and publish price, volume and time of executed transactions (post-trade transparency). Settlement must occur within prescribed timeframes, including on-DLT settlement. Fee structures must be transparent, fair and non-discriminatory and must not incentivise disorderly trading.

title: Exchange of crypto-assets for funds or other crypto-assets
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers that exchange crypto-assets for funds or for other crypto-assets must establish a non-discriminatory commercial policy that indicates, in particular, the types of clients with whom they agree to transact and the conditions to be met by those clients. They must publish either a firm price for the crypto-assets they offer to exchange, or the method they use to determine that price. Providers must execute client orders at the prices displayed at the time the order is received, or, where applicable, at the price determined by the published method, taking into account the limits set. They must publish the details of the orders and transactions they conclude, including transaction volumes and prices, where they execute transactions on their own account against clients. This post-trade transparency obligation applies unless ESMA standards provide otherwise. The aim is to ensure clients are treated fairly and transparently and can assess pricing before exchanging crypto-assets.

title: Execution of orders for crypto-assets on behalf of clients
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers executing orders on behalf of clients must take all necessary steps to obtain, when executing orders, the best possible result for their clients (best execution), taking into account price, costs, speed, likelihood of execution and settlement, size, nature and any other relevant consideration. This best-execution duty applies unless the order is executed following specific instructions from the client. CASPs must establish and implement effective execution arrangements and an order-execution policy that enables them to comply with this obligation, and must be able to demonstrate to clients, at their request, that orders were executed in accordance with that policy. They must provide appropriate information on the execution policy to clients and obtain their prior consent to it. Providers must monitor the effectiveness of their execution arrangements and policy, identify and correct deficiencies, and review the policy at least annually and whenever a material change occurs that affects their ability to obtain the best result.

title: Provision of advice on crypto-assets and of portfolio management of crypto-assets
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers giving advice on crypto-assets or providing portfolio management must assess the compatibility of the crypto-assets with the client's needs and risk tolerance. Before providing the service they must obtain information on the client's knowledge and experience, investment objectives (including risk tolerance), and financial situation including ability to bear losses (suitability assessment), and must warn the client where a crypto-asset or service is unsuitable or where the information provided is insufficient. They must inform clients whether advice is given on an independent basis and whether it is based on a broad or restricted analysis, and warn that the value of crypto-assets may fluctuate. Persons giving advice must possess the necessary knowledge and competence. Advisers must establish, maintain and implement policies and procedures to ensure they understand the nature, features and risks of the crypto-assets they assess. CASPs must provide a periodic statement to portfolio-management clients and a suitability report to advised clients, and review the suitability assessment at least every two years.

title: Provision of transfer services for crypto-assets on behalf of clients
chunk_text: Crypto-asset service providers that provide transfer services for crypto-assets on behalf of clients must enter into an agreement with the client specifying their identity, the technical operation of the transfer service, the security systems used, the types of crypto-assets covered by the transfer service, and the fees applied by the provider. The agreement must set out the duties and responsibilities of the CASP. The provision is intended to ensure clarity and protection for clients when crypto-assets are moved from one distributed-ledger address or account to another on their behalf. EBA, in close cooperation with ESMA, is mandated to issue guidelines on procedures and policies, including the rights of clients, in the context of transfer services for crypto-assets. (Note: the AML/CFT 'travel rule' obligations attaching to crypto-asset transfers are governed separately under Regulation (EU) 2023/1113, not this Article.)

title: Scope of the rules on market abuse
chunk_text: Article 86 defines the scope of MiCA's market-abuse regime (Title VI). The prohibitions and requirements apply to acts carried out by any person concerning crypto-assets that are admitted to trading on a trading platform operated by an authorised crypto-asset service provider, or for which a request for admission to trading has been made. The rules cover any transaction, order or behaviour relating to such crypto-assets, whether or not carried out on a trading platform, and apply to actions and omissions in the Union and in third countries. They do not apply to central banks, certain public bodies, or to monetary, exchange-rate or public-debt management activities carried out in the public interest. In effect, Article 86 establishes the jurisdictional and instrument-based reach of the rules on inside information, insider dealing, unlawful disclosure and market manipulation that follow.

title: Public disclosure of inside information
chunk_text: Article 88 requires issuers, offerors and persons seeking admission to trading to inform the public, as soon as possible, of inside information that directly concerns them, in a manner enabling fast access and complete, correct and timely assessment by the public. The information must be posted and maintained on the person's website for at least five years. Issuers may, on their own responsibility, delay disclosure of inside information where immediate disclosure is likely to prejudice their legitimate interests, the delay is not likely to mislead the public, and the confidentiality of the information can be ensured. ESMA is mandated to develop technical standards specifying the means and arrangements for the public disclosure and any delay of inside information. The aim is to ensure investors have equal and timely access to price-sensitive information about the relevant crypto-assets.

title: Prohibition of insider dealing
chunk_text: Article 89 prohibits insider dealing in crypto-assets. A person possessing inside information must not use that information to acquire or dispose of, directly or indirectly, the crypto-assets to which the information relates, whether for their own account or for the account of a third party. The prohibition also covers cancelling or amending an order placed before the person possessed the inside information, and using inside information to recommend, induce or otherwise cause another person to engage in such dealing. The prohibition applies to any person who possesses inside information by virtue of membership of administrative, management or supervisory bodies, holdings in the issuer, access through employment, profession or duties, or involvement in criminal activities, and more generally to anyone who knows or ought to know that the information is inside information. The provision mirrors the insider-dealing regime applicable to financial instruments, adapted to crypto-assets.

title: Prohibition of market manipulation
chunk_text: Article 91 prohibits market manipulation involving crypto-assets. It captures entering into transactions, placing orders, or engaging in other behaviour that gives, or is likely to give, false or misleading signals as to the supply, demand or price of a crypto-asset, or that secures, or is likely to secure, the price at an abnormal or artificial level, unless the conduct is for legitimate reasons and conforms to accepted market practice. It also prohibits transactions or behaviour employing fictitious devices or other forms of deception or contrivance. Further, it bans disseminating information through media or any other means that gives, or is likely to give, false or misleading signals as to a crypto-asset, including spreading rumours, where the person knew or ought to have known the information was false or misleading. The Article lists indicative examples of manipulative behaviour and tasks ESMA with developing related technical standards.

title: Register of crypto-asset white papers, of issuers of asset-referenced tokens and e-money tokens and of crypto-asset service providers
chunk_text: Article 109 requires ESMA to establish and maintain a public register. The register lists all crypto-asset white papers (and any modifications), issuers of asset-referenced tokens and e-money tokens, and authorised crypto-asset service providers, together with key information such as their name, legal form, identifiers, the services provided and the relevant competent authority. It also flags entities that have had authorisation withdrawn and includes non-compliant entities and third-country firms that have been the subject of warnings. Competent authorities and the EBA must notify ESMA of the information needed to keep the register accurate and up to date, and ESMA makes it freely accessible on its website and updates it promptly. The register provides transparency to the public and supervisors about which issuers and service providers are authorised under MiCA and the white papers that have been notified or published.

title: Administrative penalties and other administrative measures
chunk_text: Article 111 requires Member States to empower competent authorities to impose administrative penalties and other measures for breaches of MiCA, without prejudice to criminal sanctions and to supervisory powers. Penalties must be effective, proportionate and dissuasive. Available measures include public statements naming the responsible person and the breach, cease-and-desist orders, bans, disgorgement of profits gained or losses avoided, and temporary or, for repeated serious breaches, permanent bans from management functions. The Article sets minimum maximum amounts for administrative fines, expressed both as fixed sums and as percentages of total annual turnover for legal persons (and as multiples of the benefit derived where it can be determined), with thresholds varying by the type of provision breached. Competent authorities must consider aggravating and mitigating circumstances when setting penalties, and Member States may choose not to lay down administrative penalties where breaches are subject to criminal sanctions instead.
